Anaesthesia is thought to act importantly on this system: by depressing the thalamus and disrupting the thalamocortical integration and the cortical communication, anaesthetics interrupt the integrated activity necessary for consciousness — a thalamocortical switch into the unconscious state. So the thalamocortical system — the thalamus and its reciprocal loops with the cortex — relays and integrates information to the cortex and generates the rhythms of arousal and sleep; its integrated activity being regarded as central to conscious awareness, and anaesthesia acting importantly by depressing the thalamus and disrupting the thalamocortical integration — a system central to consciousness and to the action of anaesthesia.

qa.q: Describe paradoxical excitation during the induction of anaesthesia.
qa.a: During the induction of anaesthesia with some agents, a phase of excitation may paradoxically occur before the depression of consciousness, and understanding this paradoxical excitation is worth having, as it is a recognised phenomenon of importance during induction. One might expect anaesthetic agents, which depress the brain, to produce a smooth, progressive depression; but during induction, particularly with some agents, a transient phase of excitation — increased movement, sometimes with involuntary movements, and increased activity — may occur before the patient settles into anaesthesia. This paradoxical excitation is thought to arise because the anaesthetic, at the light concentrations early in induction, first depresses the inhibitory systems — or particular inhibitory neurons — before it depresses the excitatory ones; the early loss of inhibition releases, or disinhibits, other neurons, producing a transient net increase in activity and the excitation, until the deepening anaesthesia depresses these too and consciousness is lost. A similar disinhibition is described historically in the classical stages of ether anaesthesia, as a stage of excitement between the initial analgesia and the surgical anaesthesia. The phenomenon is of practical importance during induction, when the excitation must be managed and not mistaken for light anaesthesia requiring stimulation. So paradoxical excitation is a transient phase of increased movement and activity during the induction of anaesthesia, arising because the agent first depresses inhibitory neurons, disinhibiting others and transiently increasing activity, before the deepening anaesthesia depresses these too and consciousness is lost; corresponding to the classical stage of excitement — a recognised phenomenon of practical importance during induction.

qa.q: Thank you. An excellent and complete account. Let us stop there. Before we close, here are the take-home messages. First, The EEG records, from the scalp, the summed synchronised postsynaptic activity of populations of cortical neurons (not individual action potentials), paced by thalamocortical loops and modulated by the arousal system; it becomes fast/low-amplitude in alertness and slow/high-amplitude with drowsiness, sleep and anaesthesia. Second, Frequency bands: beta (>~13 Hz, alert/active), alpha (~8-13 Hz, relaxed wakefulness, eyes closed), theta (~4-8 Hz, drowsiness), delta (<~4 Hz, deep sleep — or, in the waking adult, pathology/deep anaesthesia). Activity slows and grows as the brain is depressed; very deep anaesthesia → burst suppression → isoelectric. Third, Wakefulness is maintained by the ascending arousal system (monoaminergic, cholinergic, histaminergic, orexin neurons activating cortex/thalamus); sleep is actively promoted by hypothalamic centres inhibiting it — a mutually-inhibiting switch, timed by a homeostatic sleep drive and the circadian clock. Consciousness = arousal (brainstem arousal system + thalamocortical projections) + awareness (integrated activity of widespread cortical/thalamic networks). Fourth, General anaesthesia (unconsciousness, amnesia, immobility) acts chiefly on specific protein targets — enhancing GABA-A inhibition (most IV/volatile agents) and inhibiting excitation incl. the NMDA receptor (ketamine, nitrous oxide, xenon) — depressing the thalamocortical/arousal systems of consciousness (and the cord for immobility); the older Meyer-Overton lipid theory is superseded. Depth is monitored by clinical/autonomic signs (unreliable under blockade), end-tidal agent concentration (MAC), and processed EEG monitors (e.g. bispectral index) — to avoid awareness. Thank you. That concludes the station.
